coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive monitoring of PCB77 in environmental samples using a visible-driven photoelectrochemical sensing platform coupling with exonuclease I assisted in target recycling

coredata.dc:description: Due to the urgent need for detecting trace amounts of 3,3′,4,4′-tetrachlorobiphenyl (PCB77) in the environment, we have developed an efficient and visible-driven photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing platform based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified titanium dioxide nanorods (TiO2 NRs), coupling with exonuclease I (Exo I) assisted in target recycling for significant signal amplification. CQDs/TiO2 NRs with high visible-light absorption ability and electron-hole separation efficiency is used as photoactive substrate for anchoring anti-PCB77 aptamer and its complementary DNA (cDNA). With the addition of PCB77, the specific interaction between PCB77 and its aptamer forces aptamer to separate from the electrode surface, resulting in an increase in photocurrent density. Adding Exo I in the test system, a self-catalytic target cycle was motivated, which significantly increased the PEC signal by more than twice, achieving signal amplification. The relationship between the photocurrent density changes and the concentrations of PCB77 are utilized to achieve quantitative detection of PCB77. The designed PEC sensing platform has good analytical performance with a detection limit as low as 0.33 pg L−1, high selectivity and stability. Moreover, the PEC sensor is successfully used to evaluate the content of PBC77 in the environment samples. The established sensing platform provides a simple and efficient method for detecting trace amounts of PCB77 in the environment.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive monitoring of PCB77 in environmental samples using a visible-driven photoelectrochemical sensing platform coupling with exonuclease I assisted in target recycling

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the urgent need for detecting trace amounts of 3,3′,4,4′-tetrachlorobiphenyl (PCB77) in the environment, we have developed an efficient and visible-driven photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing platform based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified titanium dioxide nanorods (TiO2 NRs), coupling with exonuclease I (Exo I) assisted in target recycling for significant signal amplification. CQDs/TiO2 NRs with high visible-light absorption ability and electron-hole separation efficiency is used as photoactive substrate for anchoring anti-PCB77 aptamer and its complementary DNA (cDNA). With the addition of PCB77, the specific interaction between PCB77 and its aptamer forces aptamer to separate from the electrode surface, resulting in an increase in photocurrent density. Adding Exo I in the test system, a self-catalytic target cycle was motivated, which significantly increased the PEC signal by more than twice, achieving signal amplification. The relationship between the photocurrent density changes and the concentrations of PCB77 are utilized to achieve quantitative detection of PCB77. The designed PEC sensing platform has good analytical performance with a detection limit as low as 0.33 pg L−1, high selectivity and stability. Moreover, the PEC sensor is successfully used to evaluate the content of PBC77 in the environment samples. The established sensing platform provides a simple and efficient method for detecting trace amounts of PCB77 in the environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs) are a class of highly toxic and persistent organic pollutants (Varshney et al., 2024). They are usually resistant to chemical, biological, and radiation degradation, displaying long-term stability in the environment (Lee et al., 2022; Wilsey et al., 2023), as well as often be found in various environmental media (soil, sediment, atmosphere, organisms and water). Due to their stable accumulation and high toxicity in living organisms (Spognardi et al., 2021), PCBs pose a serious threat to the environment and human health (Mohammadi et al., 2020; Scatena et al., 2019). The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) in US has set a maximum PCBs content of 0.5 μg L−1 in drinking water (Ahmad et al., 2019). Meanwhile, numerous studies have identified that PCBs that replace the non ortho position of hydrogen atoms on the phenyl ring are more toxic among the 209 homologous compounds in PCBs family (Yilmaz et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2022). 3,3′,4,4′-Tetrachlorobiphenyl (PCB77) as one of coplanar PCBs exhibits dioxin-like toxicity (Gao et al., 2020), which has serious negative effects on the environment and humans even at a very low concentration in the environment. Therefore, developing a fast, highly reliable, and ultra-sensitive PCB77 detection technology is crucial for enhancing environmental safety.

So far, commonly used instrumental analysis methods for the detection of PCB77 include liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Geng et al., 2016), gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) (Alsefri et al., 2023; Li et al., 2021). Although these instrument methods have excellent sensitivity and accuracy, they often require expensive instruments, cumbersome sample processing, and professional personnel (Gao et al., 2024; Lu et al., 2023). A series of simpler and more affordable enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Verdian et al., 2019), surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) techniques (Cheng et al., 2020; Cheng et al., 2018; Sultangaziyev et al., 2022), and fluorescence and electrochemical sensors (Han et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021) have been developed for the detection of PCB77. However, antibodies as recognition elements in commercial ELISA kits, have some limitations in terms of stability, screening costs, and production time (Javidi et al., 2018); SERS technology also has drawbacks such as weak adsorption capacity and low efficiency of target molecules (Zhang et al., 2020). The drawbacks limit their practical application. More importantly, the individual homologues of PCBs are extremely low in the environment (Zhu et al., 2022). Moreover, the existence of a large number of other high-concentration organic compounds seriously threatens the accurate determination of individual PCBs in complex environment. Therefore, it is a challenging task to develop a simple, high sensitivity and high selectivity for low-concentration PCB77 detection. Photoelectrochemical (PEC) technology is an emerging and promising analytical method that has been widely studied due to its fast response, high sensitivity, simple operation, real-time detection, low cost, and ease of miniaturization (Chen et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2020; Shen et al., 2022). Furthermore, PEC technique possesses high sensitivity resulting from the complete separation of photo-excitation and electrochemical detection reduces the background signal (Gao et al., 2023; Lin et al., 2023). But its main disadvantage is the lack of selectivity since excess strong oxidants produced during light excitation can oxidize most of pollutants in the environment. Aptamer, an oligonucleotide sequence can specifically identify its target with sufficient affinity (Cai et al., 2018; Qiu et al., 2018). Therefore, the aptamer-based PEC sensors can undoubtedly achieve high selectivity in the determination of PCB77 in the environment. However, compared to biological macromolecules, the biological recognition interaction between small pollutant molecules and aptamer is usually weaker, resulting in less significant conformational changes of aptamer molecules (Shen et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2022). Therefore, it is very difficult to achieve accurate and ultrasensitive detection of extremely low amounts of PCB77 in the environment through such weak detection signals. Exonuclease I (Exo I) can effectively degrade nucleotides in single stranded DNA from 3′ to 5′ (Chen et al., 2020). It has been widely used in the design of various sensors with high sensitivity, playing a role in signal amplification (Lv et al., 2019; Qiu et al., 2017). For instance, Ji et al. proposed an electrochemical biosensor for detecting carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) using Exo III. When CEA was present, the hairpin DNA was opened and digested by Exo III to release the target mimic (T1) to participate in the next cycle, amplifying the detection signal and achieving ultra-sensitive detection (Ji et al., 2022). Zhu et al. developed a PEC sensor based on Exo I for detecting listeria monocytogenes (Zhu et al., 2021). By utilizing the shear effect of Exo I, the signal changes before and after the addition of pathogenic bacteria were distinctly amplified. Inspired by these works, Exo I is attempted to introduce into PEC sensing system to amplify detection signal and achieve ultra-sensitive detection of PCB77.

In addition, it is very crucial to select an appropriate photoactive material to immobilize aptamer molecules and achieve excellent sensing performance. So far, TiO2 has been widely used in PEC sensing fields due to its excellent stability, environmental friendliness, and low cost (Lan et al., 2022). Among them, one-dimensional TiO2 nanorods (TiO2 NRs) have attracted more interest due to their large surface area and excellent electrical transport performance (Wang et al., 2022), compared with other TiO2 nanostructures. However, the low light utilization efficiency and high photogenerated carriers recombination rate of TiO2 greatly limit its further application (Sohn et al., 2022). Numerous studies have confirmed that it is a favorable strategy for modifying TiO2 with narrow-band semiconductors to broaden its visible-light absorption range and PEC activity (Cai et al., 2020; Shu et al., 2018). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have the characteristics of wide absorption spectrum, non-toxicity, stable chemical properties, and good biocompatibility, making them a green alternative to toxic transition metal quantum dots (Ikram et al., 2022). Therefore, in the work, CQDs modified TiO2 NRs (CQDs/TiO2 NRs) composites were designed as the photoactive substrate. A novel and visible-driven PEC sensor was developed by anchoring aptamer and its complementary DNA (cDNA) on the composites for highly sensitive and selective determination of PCB77 in the environment. The designed CQDs/TiO2 NRs composites display strong visible light absorption ability and excellent photocurrent response. Furthermore, owing to its large surface area and good biocompatibility, it can provide a larger anchoring space for biomolecules, and maintain good biological activity and binding ability, further improving the sensing performance of the sensor. During PEC measuring, aptamer molecules specifically bind to PCB77 and detach from the electrode surface, leading to an increase in PEC response. With the help of Exo I, the aptamer hydrolysis in the complexes and the target PCB77 cycle are initiated to achieve signal amplification. Therefore, PCB77 can be quantified by the variation of photocurrent with the concentration of PCB77, ranging from 0.003 ng L−1 to 5 ng L−1. Meanwhile, the sensing mechanism of PEC sensing platform is comprehensively explored and its application in actual environmental samples is investigated.

Graphite rods, 2,3′,5,5′-tetrachlorobiphenyl (PCB72), 3,3′,4,4′-tetrachlorobiphenyl (PCB77), 3,4,4′,5-tetrachlorobiphenyl (PCB81) and 3,3′,4,4′,5-pentachlorobiphenyl (PCB126) were bought from Innochem (Beijing, China). Tetrabutyl titanate (C16H36O4Ti) was supplied by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) conducting glasses (20 mm × 50 mm) were bought from Wuhan Jing Solar Energy Technology Co., Ltd. Anti-PCB77 aptamer probe was ordered from Shanghai Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China) and the sequences were given below:
Aptamer: 5′-GGCGGGGCTACGAAGTAGTGATTTTTTCCGATGGCCCGTG-(CH2)6-3′
cDNA: 5′-TTCGTAGCCCCGCCTTTTTTTTTTTT-(CH2)6-NH2-3′

PEC and electrochemical experiments were conducted at CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (CHI, Shanghai, China) using a traditional three-electrode system with an improved FTO as the working electrode, a platinum plate as the counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. The surface morphology of the prepared materials was studied using scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were used to characterize the microstructure, surface elemental composition, and crystal structure of composite materials and performed at 200 kV using a JSM-7001F (JEOL, Japan) with an energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX). The particle size of the materials were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS, 90Plus/BI-MAS, USA) at 25 °C. X-ray photoelectron energy spectra (XPS) experiments were operated on AXIS ULTRA DLD (Kratos, British). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were carried out on D8 Venture (Bruker, Germany). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the materials were performed on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (U-3010, Hitachi, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra were performed on Aligent Cary 8454 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on a fluorescence spectrometer under excitation wavelengths of 320–440 nm (Jasco, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy of materials were performed on a tensor 27 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany), using high-purity KBr compression as the standard reagent to establish a baseline. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were measured at different scanning rates (10 mV s−1–90 mV s−1) by the electrochemical double-layer capacitance method to estimate the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA).

According to the reported literature (Liu and Aydil, 2009) with slight modifications, firstly, FTO was sonicated with acetone, deionized water, and absolute ethanol for 20 min each in sequence. Subsequently, 0.26 mL tetrabutyl titanate was added to the solution containing 10 mL ultrapure water and 10 mL of concentrated hydrochloric acid and stirred for 5 min. Then, the above solution was transferred to 50 mL high-pressure reaction kettle and placed conductive surface of FTO on the tank wall of the reaction kettle, following by reacting for 6 h under 180 °C. After cooled naturally to room temperature, as-synthesized TiO2 NRs/FTO was annealed at 450 °C for 1 h to prepare crystallized TiO2 NRs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 NRs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, two graphite-carbon rods were washed multiple times with ultrapure water to remove surface impurities. After natural drying, a cleaned graphite-carbon rod was inserted into the ultrapure water as the anode (100 mL), placed parallel to the other graphite rod as the counter-electrode with a separation of 7.5 cm. The electrolytic reaction was continuously for 120 h at an applied voltage of 60 V until the color of the solution changed from colorless to yellow brown, and finally to black brown. Then, the obtained solution was filtered and centrifuged at 15,000 r min−1 for 30 min to remove large particulate matter. Finally, the synthesized CQDs aqueous solution was drained of water using a rotary evaporator to obtain a solid CQDs.

The prepared TiO2 NRs (3 cm × 2 cm) as the working electrode, was placed in a mixed solution containing 10 mL citric acid sodium citrate buffer solution (0.1 M, pH = 4.5) and 10 mL concentrated CDs solution (0.1 mg mL−1). A platinum plate (3 cm × l.5 cm) served as the counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl was used as the reference electrode. After depositing at a potential of −1.1 V for 20 min, CQDs/TiO2 NRs were obtained.

Firstly, CQDs/TiO2 NRs was fully sealed with insulating adhesive, and only 1.0 cm2 area was retained. 35 μL chitosan solution (1 %, w/v) was covered onto the sealed composites with the area of 1.0 cm2, then 45 μL of 3 % glutaraldehyde solution was added and reacted for 30 min at room temperature. After rinsed with deionized water, 30 μL cDNA (3 μM) was dropped on aldehyde-functionalized CQDs/TiO2 NRs for overnight at 4 °C. The aptamer-immobilized CQDs/TiO2 NRs were rinsed to remove unbound aptamer using Tris-HCl (pH 7.41) solution, and then there was blocked with 30 μL bovine serum albumin (BSA) solution (3 %, w/v) to avoid non-specific adsorption. The PEC sensing platform was prepared and stored at 4 °C for use.

The PEC experiment was conducted using chronoamperometry in a 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.41) under visible-light irradiation at a potential of 0 V. A 300 W Xe lamp (Beijing, China) with a 420 nm cut-off filter was chosen as the light source with an intensity of 50 mW cm−2. The interval between the irradiation source and the photoelectrode is 30 cm. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were tested in a solution containing 5.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] and 0.1 M KCl in the frequency range of 10−2 Hz–105 Hz at 5 mV.

When detecting PCB77, the sensor was incubated in 30.0 μL of PCB77 with different concentrations ranging from 0.003 ng L−1 to 30 ng L−1. Then, 20 U Exo I and 10 × Reaction buffer were added to the above solution, and stirred slightly to ensure that the reaction was completed. The corresponding PEC density was recorded in a 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 7.41).

Fenhe water and soil were selected as the actual samples to study the practicability of the sensing platform. The water samples were first filtered through ordinary filter paper to remove suspended particles and other solid impurities. The filtrate was filtered through the 0.22 μm filter membrane again. The final sample filtrate was added to 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.41) electrolyte solution. The collected soil samples were prepared by the previously reported method with slight modifications (Shi et al., 2018). After the collected soil was naturally dried, and it continued to be grinded to pass through a 60 mesh sieve. Further treatment with concentrated sulfuric acid can remove unstable coexisting substances such as organochlorine pesticides from the soil, achieving preliminary purification of the soil samples. Three different concentrations of PCB77 of 0.05 ng L−1, 0.1 ng L−1 and 1.0 ng L−1 were added to these samples respectively and detected for three parallel experiments.

Fig. 1A and B show the TEM images of CQDs at different sizes. As shown in Fig. 1A, CQDs is well dispersed, and the particle size range of CQDs is 1.5 nm to 5.5 nm according to the DLS histogram (Inset of Fig. 1A). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image shows a lattice spacing of approximately 0.321 nm (Inset of Fig. 1B), which is well consistent with the (002) lattice plane of graphite carbon (Niu et al., 2020). This preliminary indicates that CQDs have been successfully synthesized through the electrolysis of graphite-carbon rods.

Fig. 1C shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of as-synthesized CQDs. It can be clearly observed that CQDs exhibit strong and wide absorption in the UV and visible regions. The initial peak located at approximately 300 nm represents the typical absorption of aromatic pi system (Ming et al., 2012), which is attributed to the absorption of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. In order to further investigate the optical properties of CQDs, the PL studies were conducted using different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 1D). Under excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 nm to 440 nm, CQDs exhibited different PL emission wavelengths, demonstrating an excitation-dependent PL behavior. Meanwhile, PL spectra displayed that some PL peaks extended to the visible light region, and the redshift can be attributed to the quantum confinement effect (Vibhute et al., 2022), in which the conduction band and valence band moved in opposite directions.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of TiO2 NRs in-situ grown on the surface of FTO through a one-step hydrothermal method is shown in Fig. 2A. It can be clearly seen that TiO2 uniformly covers FTO in a columnar pattern with an average diameter of 95 ± 5 nm. There are lots of gaps between the nanorods, providing a relatively large space for the deposition of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2B, the SEM image of TiO2 loaded with CQDs shows that its morphology has not changed, which may be due to the relatively small size of CQDs. In order to further verify the successful modification of CQDs on TiO2 NRs, TEM was used to investigate the changes in the morphology of the composites. Fig. 2C shows the TEM image of TiO2 NRs, and it clearly displays a rod-shaped structure and smooth surface. The HRTEM image (Inset of Fig. 2C) shows a lattice spacing of 0.35 nm, corresponding to the (101) crystal plane of rutile TiO2 (Ding et al., 2022). From the TEM image of the composites in Fig. 2D, CQDs can be clearly observed (the yellow circles). This suggests tentatively that CQDs can be attached to TiO2 NRs without any agglomeration through electrodeposition technology. The load of CQDs on TiO2 NRs will enhances the utilization of visible light, and reduces the recombination of electron-hole pairs, greatly promoting the PEC response. The EDS analysis in Fig. 2E demonstrates the existence of C, O and Ti in the CQDs/TiO2 NRs with the atomic percentages of 4.54 %, 84.24 %, and 11.22 %, respectively (Table S1). In addition, the elemental mappings demonstrate the uniform distribution of C, O, and Ti on CQDs/TiO2 NRs. This further proves the successful load of CQDs on TiO2 NRs.

Furthermore, the presence of elements and chemical states of CQDs/TiO2 NRs was further studied with XPS. As shown in Fig. 3A, The XPS full spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 NRs indicates the presence of C, O and Ti elements. Meanwhile, the high-resolution spectra of the three elements are displayed in Fig. 3B–D. In Fig. 3B, the binding energy peaks of 458.5 eV and 464.2 eV belong to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, demonstrating that Ti in CQDs/TiO2 NRs composites shows positive tetravalent (Moon and Yong, 2020). For the high-resolution spectra of C1s in Fig. 3C, the peak at 285.2 eV belongs to the CC bond of CQDs, and the other peaks at 286.8 eV, 287.4 eV and 288.9 eV are ascribed to CH, CO and CO, respectively (Ming et al., 2012). For the high-resolution spectra of O1s in Fig. 3D, two peaks are showed at 529.9 eV and 531.8 eV are attributed to the surface lattice oxygen and hydroxyl oxygen in TiO2 NRs (Deng et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2021).

The XRD patterns of the prepared composites are shown in Fig. 4A and B. The diffraction peaks at 18.64°, 22.86° (signed with ▽) and 26.25° are observed from the XRD pattern of CQDs in Fig. 4A, corresponding to the (103) plane of graphite (belonging to hexagonal carbon), amorphous carbon, and its (002) plane, respectively (Ming et al., 2012). Fig. 4B shows the XRD patterns of TiO2 NRs (curve a) and CQDs/TiO2 NRs (curve b). From curve a, three different diffraction peaks at 35.94°, 62.66°, and 69.75° correspond to the (101), (002), and (112) crystal planes, respectively, demonstrating rutile phase of TiO2 (JCPDS No. 21-1276) (Yan et al., 2023). Also, there is a new characteristic peak at 26.25° in the XRD pattern of CQDs/TiO2 NRs (curve b), corresponding to the planes of (002) of the graphite plane. These results further demonstrate that CQDs have been successfully decorated on TiO2 NRs.

FT-IR measurements were performed to analyze the functional groups present in CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 NRs in Fig. 4C. For individual CQDs, the wide absorption peak at 3439 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of OH and NH groups in CQDs. The absorption peaks at 1637 cm−1 and 1420 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CO and CN, respectively. These absorption peaks have confirmed that the presence of amide, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of CQDs. For the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 NRs, in addition to the characteristic peaks of CQDs, there is a new peak at about 500 cm−1. that is due to the stretching vibration peak of TiOTi in TiO2, further confirming the successful synthesis of CQDs/TiO2 NRs.

Fig. 4D shows the DRS of the as-prepared samples. TiO2 NRs have strong ultraviolet light absorption ability, and its absorption edge is located at 398 nm. After depositing CQDs onto TiO2 NRs, it is observed that the absorption edge of the composites shifts red, enhancing the absorption of visible light. The results indicate that the modification of CQDs on TiO2 NRs can effectively enhance visible-light absorption ability and suppress the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes, improving PEC activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and characterization of the proposed PEC sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 demonstrates the assembly process of the PEC sensing platform. Chitosan and glutaraldehyde solution are used as crosslinking agents to make the surface of CQDs/TiO2 NRs functionalize with aldehyde groups (CHO). Subsequently, terminal-amino modified cDNA is immobilized on the composites through condensation reactions between NH2 and CHO. Then, anti-PCB77 aptamer molecules are hybridized with cDNA and introduced into the electrode to form dsDNA. Fig. 5A shows the photocurrent response of the different construction processes of the PEC aptasensor. Under the irradiation of visible light, the photocurrent density of TiO2 NRs is only 3.4 μA/cm2 (curve a). After TiO2 NRs decorated with CQDs, the photocurrent density increases to 12.4 μA/cm2 (curve b), confirming that coupling CQDs with TiO2 NRs can significantly reduce carriers recombination and improve PEC response. The current is further increased to 13.6 μA/cm2 when cDNA is anchored on the electrode (curve c), which may be due to the weakening of the Fermi level arrangement of hybrid structures of CQDs/TiO2 NRs by DNA fixation (Gao et al., 2020). After aptamer molecules are modified, the PEC response sharply decreases to 7.6 μA/cm2 (curve d). The decrease in photocurrent density is attributed to the formation of a more rigid double helix dsDNA, which hinders electron transfer. When the PEC sensor is incubated for 40 min in a solution containing 3 pg L−1 PCB77, and the photocurrent density increases slightly to 8.3 μA/cm2 (curve e). This is because the specific binding of the aptamer towards PCB77 is much greater than the hybrid between aptamer and its cDNA, resulting in the separation of aptamer molecules from composites surface. So it leads to an increase of the photocurrent density. While 20 U Exo I is added, the photocurrent increases to 9.8 μA/cm2 again (curve f). This is ascribed to the fact that the aptamer-PCB77 complexes are digested by Exo I and release PCB77 to participate in the next reaction. Thus, more and more aptamer molecules fall from the electrode surface, further increasing the photocurrent density. Therefore, the significant changes in photocurrent density can be used for the quantitative detection of PCB77.

Fig. 5B is EIS of each step during constructing PEC aptasensor. The fitting circuit includes the double-layer capacitance (Cdl), the solution-phase resistance (Rs) and the electron transfer resistance (Rct). The Rct of CQDs/TiO2 NRs is approximately 533.6 Ω (curve b), which is lower than that of pure TiO2 NRs (curve a). When cDNA is modified (curve c), the Rct value increases significantly to about 1136.0 Ω. After modifying aptamer molecules, the Rct value further increases to 3480.8 Ω (curve d). Because the formation of DNA double helix structure with poor conductivity hinders the electron transfer between the electrode and the solution. After adding PCB77, the Rct value decreases to approximately 2356.0 Ω (curve e). This can be explained by the release of aptamer molecules from the electrode surface as its strong affinity for PCB77, leading to a decrease in electron transfer resistance. When 20 U Exo I is added, the Rct value continues to decrease to approximately 2103.2 Ω (curve f). Therefore, the EIS results are well consistent with the I-t responses, indicating that the constructed sensor is practicable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of the PEC sensing platform for PCB77
ce:section.ce:para: After adding PCB77, aptamer molecules were isolated from dsDNA anchored on the electrode due to the specific recognition of aptamer to PCB77. However, the formed aptamer-PCB77 complexes fell into the test solution and were then digested by Exo I, releasing PCB77 to proceed to the next cycle. Therefore, due to the release of aptamer molecules from the electrode surface, resulting in a significant increase in photocurrent density. Meanwhile, the released PCB77 will bind with other aptamer molecules to continuously trigger the next dissociation and digestion. In this way, a small amount of PCB77 in the presence of Exo I can trigger signal production several times, thus achieving signal amplification and dramatically improving the analytical behavior of the sensing platform. In order to confirm the signal amplification effect of Exo I during the detection, the analytical behavior of the PEC sensor before and after the introduction of Exo I were detected (Fig. 5C). In blank solution, the photocurrent density of the sensor was approximately 7.6 μA/cm2 (curve a). When the sensor was incubated in the solution containing only 3 pg L−1 PCB77, the photocurrent density slightly increased to 8.3 μA/cm2 (curve b). The increment in photocurrent density (ΔI0) was 0.7 μA/cm2. After introducing Exo I into the PEC sensing system, Exo I stimulated aptamer hydrolysis in PCB77-aptamer complexes, releasing the target PCB77 that continued to bind to other aptamer molecules on the electrode. At this point, the photocurrent signal significantly increased to 9.8 μA/cm2 (curve c). The change in photocurrent density (ΔIi) is 1.5 μA/cm2, which was two times more than that of only PCB77. The results confirmed that the introduction of Exo I greatly improved the photocurrent response of the sensor, playing a role in signal amplification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Highly sensitive PEC detection of PCB77
ce:section.ce:para: The photocurrent responses at different concentrations of PCB77 were measured to evaluate the analytical performance of the sensor using I-t technology, as shown in Fig. 6A. It is observed that as the concentrations of PCB77 increase from 0.003 ng L−1 to 5 ng L−1, the photocurrent density gradually increase. However, when the concentration of PCB77 exceeds 5 ng L−1, there is almost no significant change in the photocurrent density. This indicates that aptamer molecules on the electrode have reacted completely with PCB77. As shown in Fig. 6B, ΔI/I0 and the log of PCB77 concentrations (logC) has well linear in the concentration range of 0.003 ng L−1 to 5 ng L−1 (Inset of Fig. 6B), where ΔI is the photocurrent density change after (Ii) and before (I0) PCB77 recognition (ΔI = Ii-I0). The fitting linear equation is ΔI/I0 = 1.306 + 0.4835 logC (R2 = 0.9952) and the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated as 0.33 pg L−1 based on 3σ/S (where σ represents the standard deviation of the measured value for the blank sample, S represents the slope of the linear equation). Another important parameter quantification limit (LOQ) can be determined by a 10:1 signal-to-noise ratio (Joshi et al., 2022). Therefore, the LOQ value of each PEC sensor is approximately 3.3 times its LOD value. So the LOQ of the sensing platform is calculated to be 1.089 pg L−1. Compared with the previous methods for detection of PCB77, the comparison results are listed in Table 1 . Obviously, the proposed PEC sensor has a wider detection range and a lower detection limit, indicating its potential in the determination of trace amounts of PCB77. The overwhelming analytical behavior of the PEC sensor is mainly owing to the following reasons. Firstly, CQDs/TiO2 NRs composites are designed as an excellent photoactive material for loading cDNA and aptamer molecules. The composites exhibit strong visible-light absorption ability and high PEC response, which is beneficial for the sensing behavior. Besides, CQDs/TiO2 NRs with large surface area and excellent biocompatibility is more advantageous for anchoring biomolecules and maintaining their excellent biological activity and strong affinity for PCB77, further improving the sensing performance of the sensor. To further demonstrate the superiority of the composites, the CV curves were measured to estimate the ECSA of the electrodes, as detailed in Fig. S1. In addition, the biocompatibility of the electrodes were studied using the contact angle measurements, as shown in Fig. S2.

In addition, prior to analysis, some parameters that affect the sensing performance were optimized in order to accomplish the prominent sensing performance of the PEC sensor. The amount of CQDs loaded on TiO2 NRs is a key factor, which mainly depends on the deposition time of CQDs. So the optimal deposition time of CQDs was first explored by different techniques and the detailed information was provided in Figs. S3–S4. The optimal deposition time was selected as 20 min. Also, the cDNA concentrations related to the loading amount of aptamer molecules and the incubation time of the PEC sensor in PCB77 solution were also investigated, and the related curves are shown in Figs. S5 and S6. The results indicate that the cDNA concentration of 3 μM is the best and the best incubation time is selected as 40 min. Besides, the optimal concentration for Exo I is 20 U (Fig. S7), and 120 min was chosen as the optimal enzymatic hydrolysis time for Exo I (Fig. S8).

To further explore the selectivity of the sensor, some substances that coexist or have similar structure with PCB77, including PCB81, PCB72, PCB126, 2,4-D and atrazine are chosen as the interferents. Herein, the concentration of PCB77 is 1.0 ng L−1, and the concentration of PCB81, PCB72, PCB126, 2,4-D, and Atrazine are 100 times more abundant than PCB77. And the structural formula of PCB77 and five different interferents are shown in Fig. S9. The relative change of the photocurrent density, ΔI/ΔI0 for two-component system containing one of five different interferents and 1.0 ng L−1 PCB77, and their mixture were utilized to evaluate the selectivity of the sensor. ΔI0 refers to the photocurrent density change after and before incubation in 1.0 ng L−1 PCB77. And ΔI is the change of photocurrent density after and before adding 100 ng L−1 one among five interfering substances in the solution containing 1.0 ng L−1 PCB77. As indicated in Fig. 7A, the photocurrent response is only obvious when PCB77 has been measured. But when the interferents were added to the solution already containing PCB77, the photocurrent changed very little. Meanwhile, the photocurrent response containing PCB77 and all interferents was also determined, and the results showed that it was close to the photocurrent change that only detected PCB77. The results show that the sensing platform have good selectivity and anti-interference capability.

The stability is another key factor to evaluate the performance of the sensing platform. The photoexcited-stability was studied by repeatedly turning on and off the excitation light. As shown in Fig. 7B, it is observed that the photocurrent density is almost no change over 2000 s, indicating the good stability of the PEC aptasensor. Long-term stability of the sensing platform was also investigated by comparing the changes in photocurrent response stored for 2 weeks at 4 °C. The result shows that the photocurrent response remains 95.6 % of its initial value, demonstrating a good long-term stability of the sensing platform.

In addition, five identical sensing platforms were constructed to detect 0.1 ng L−1 PCB77 under the same conditions for verifying the reproducibility of the PEC sensing platform. As shown in Fig. 7C. The difference between these five sensing platforms was very small, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.12 %. Meanwhile, the same sensing platform was used to measure seven times in parallel in a 0.1 ng L−1 PCB77 solution, the RSD was only 1.37 %. These results indicated that the constructed PEC sensing platform had good repeatability and reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Environmental samples analysis using PEC aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The ultrasensitivity, high selectivity and excellent stability of the developed PEC aptasensor suggest it can be applied to the analysis of PCB77 in real samples. Due to the large-scale use of PCB77, the constructed PEC aptasensor has been used to detect the concentration of PCB77 in the Fenhe River and the soil along the Fenhe River. The recovery results of PCB77 in two different samples are shown in Table 2 . In addition, the accuracy and reliability of the proposed method was verified by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). Seen from Table 2, the analytical results of the established sensor are consistent with HPLC. The recovery rate of both methods is 93.9 %–103.0 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is less than 5.6 %. The result demonstrates the practicality and reliability of the present aptasensor for the detection of PCB77 in actual environmental samples. What's more, the present method is simpler, faster and cheaper than HPLC, without complex sample treatment, professional test and high priced instruments.

In summary, an efficient and visible-driven PEC sensing platform was developed based on CQDs/TiO2 NRs for ultrasensitive detection of PCB77. Through the introduction of Exo I, playing an important role in signal amplification, the sensing performance has been greatly improved. So the proposed sensor showed high sensitivity, specificity and stability in the detection of PCB77. Besides, it had been successfully used to determine trace PCB77 levels in environmental samples. The established PEC sensing platform could provide a simple and effective strategy for the detection of PCBs in the environment, which would have the potential to become a powerful means to evaluate the content of PCBs in the environment.

Lining Su: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Guizhen Wang: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Linlin Zhao: Investigation, Data curation. Yuan Deng: Data curation, Formal analysis. Yujing Guo: Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Yong Xiao: Supervision, Methodology. Huanwen Wang: Supervision, Methodology. Chuan Dong: Investigation, Data curation. Lifang Fan: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization.
